The beautiful platform has no annoying ads, you can easily get popular, and everyone is very interested to each other.

I'm gonna miss you Mr. Jingles.

comment:
Hello, Google+ veteran and refugee here - been on the site since 2015.

Just here to say that, man, I still miss that site.
I've made so many friends there, so many memories, spent so much time.. Just to see it gone is heartbreaking.
Every morning, I would still open up G+ just to see what people are posting (mostly memes about the shut down) only to just see empty space and Google+ themselves talking about the shutdown itself.

It was such a small community, a tightly-knit one at that.

I don't think I'll ever forget that site. Mostly because, well, since it wasn't as popular, there wasn't much competition like Instagram nor was it as 'agrresive' as Twitter. Small yet amazing artists thrived there and actually got attention, people used slurs daily (i.g. the n word) because none of us really cared, hell, you could make meaningful relationships with others and not just rely on exisiting ones. We would have our own inside jokes, classic G+ posts that somehow made their way onto other social medias. Google+ literally saved me from ending my own life, and it saved others, too.

For example, a person on there - don't remember his name - he looked to be the average G+ (average to us, atleast). He would always post memes and whatnot but he would mostly make posts where he would always gush on about birds and how cute they were. I still remember the countless times where he would post vents about his depression, suicide, life problems, saying that Google+ has been making his life worthwhile, etc. So, when he finally announced that he was leaving G+, I felt. Sad. (this was two years before the shut down was announced) Yes, he listed his social medias, though I only followed him on IG for whatever reason. Time skip to today, he still hasn't posted anything on Instagram. I'm worried that he might've already comitted suicide.

Before the shutdown, I swear, it was the most saddest moment I've seen on G+. People were saying their goodbyes, listing their social medias so they could stay connected. Some were making memes about our inevitable doom to try and lighten it up, some were begging Google to not delete it. 'Course, everyone made face reveals, too, as I did myself. And when it shutted down it was just.. Absolutely heartbreaking.

It really felt like losing a tightly-knit community.

I miss it, I really do.
I want to be mad at Google so much, but I can't. Since they were the ones who brought G+ to life in the first place.

Thank you, Google+.
video_title: Why Google+ Failed

comment:
I liked to imagine G+, and the internet at large, as a wasteland. You're typically fine as long as you stick together with your little community, but if you go out and explore you'll find some really creepy, discomforting stuff.

I myself found quite a comfortable little home in a Halo RP. The moderators took it really seriously with an ongoing, original narrative that everyones' RPs had to fit into. They felt that RPing is a bit like writing a story: only you do it with other people. As such, your RPs had to be written like a story; no first-person commentary, no 'script talk'. Proper writing. I definitely miss it and the people I came to know there.

comment:
I think Google+ was a really good site, particularly before most of its features were separated into multiple apps, i.e. Hangouts. The one reason the product failed is because of Google's idiocy. Google has a knack for taking things that work, and breaking them in the name of "improving user experience", the only reason they haven't caused the death of YouTube is that YouTube has 0 competitors.  Remember Inbox and all the hype around it? A year or two later it was discontinued. 

I have never seen a more volatile tech company in my life, as far as software products go. This behaviour also extends into their developer ecosystem. They would introduce a new API for Android and tout it as "the way forward", then a few years later, they deprecate it...
video_title: What Happened to Google+? The Story Behind Googles Biggest Ever Failure

comment:
Google+ was the only social media site that I actually liked. The Circles feature was amazing, and the design was very modern compared to it's competitors at the time. A lot of casual social media users called it a ghost town, but the reality is that the Tech and Art communities were absolutely thriving on it. Uncountable numbers of posts were made daily in tech and art related groups. Ever since it shut down, I've never actively used another social media site again.

Google once again ruining a great product.
video_title: What Happened to Google+? The Story Behind Googles Biggest Ever Failure

comment:
Ima be honest, I still miss it at times. I was a very “loyal” user to it (by loyal I mean I used it a lot and thought it was good so I’d recommend it to other people and tell them about it lmao) and used it nonstop everyday 24/7. Mades loads of friends and had tons of fun, but also had tons of bad things happen such as people harassing me or friends for the dumbest things.
Unfortunately when it got deleted, I lost all that and at least 5 almost 6 years worth of art and characters as well as friends who I never could find a way to find again. 
So, it was fun while it existed, but at the same time, I’m glad that it’s gone because of all the bad shit and drama that was unnecessarily shoved into your throat for no reason.

comment: I personally think it partly failed because they overestimated how many friends we actually have..and how close we are to those “mutuals” ..most people on social media are acquaintances, not FRIENDS friends, so they were essentially forcing friendships to be closer than they really are.  Unless you were in an office setting, it just seemed like glorified office email service for people who didn’t need it.  Circles was visually awkward as well.   Only white collar workers cared for it because it’s basically a glorified office email software…it didnt appeal to the masses which are mostly blue collar people.. it needed to be more individualistic., it was TOO social for social media.

comment:
I still use G+. I am on several sites , but G+ is my favorite. There has always been a greater emphasis on culture , particularly the wonderfully diverse collections that are not to be found elsewhere. Instagram is the big city , whereas G+ is the neighborhood. I am not happy about its imminent demise and I assure you that I am not alone in this. While its usership was always far less than Facebook , the popular assertion that 'nobody' used it is patently false.
 Google itself failed to realize the value of G+ and never promoted it  properly.
